items.q: What does modulation mean in music?
items.correct: Changing from one key to another within a piece.
items.why: Students conflate modulation with any musical change. Specifically, modulation is a KEY CHANGE, usually established by a perfect cadence in the new key - a structural device.

items.q: How does a harmonic minor scale differ from a natural minor?
items.correct: The seventh degree is raised by a semitone (creating a leading note).
items.misconception: All notes are raised by a semitone.
items.why: Students think the whole scale changes. Only the 7TH is altered to create a leading note, which strengthens cadences in minor keys - producing the characteristic augmented second between 6th and 7th.

items.q: How do you identify a major key from its key signature?
items.correct: The last sharp is the leading note (semitone below tonic); the second-to-last flat is the tonic.
items.misconception: The number of sharps or flats equals the tonic note number.
items.why: Students lack a reliable rule. For sharp keys: last sharp + 1 semitone = tonic (e.g., F sharp means G major). For flat keys: the second-to-last flat IS the tonic (or memorise: 1 flat = F major).

items.q: A chord that functions in both old and new key is a:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Pivot chord. “Cadenza” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Two voices moving in parallel by a perfect fifth is:
items.why: Correct answer: Traditionally forbidden. “Standard practice” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Modulation from C major to G major moves up by how many perfect fifths?
items.why: Exact answer: 1. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
